prompt: Write a warm greeting to a new colleague in five different languages (your choice). After each, add a one-line note about what's culturally implied by the register you chose.
response:
1. English  
“Hey Alex, so glad you’re here—welcome to the team! If you need anything, just holler.”  
(The casual “hey” and “holler” signal an easy-going, flat-hierarchy culture where first-name terms and instant friendliness are the norm.)

2. Japanese  
「田中さん、ようこそ！お疲れさまです。何かございましたら、遠慮なくお知らせください。」  
(The polite –san, the humble verb forms, and the set phrase otsukaresama show respect while still offering help—standard for new coworkers in Japanese offices.)

3. Spanish  
¡Hola Laura! Qué alegría tenerte con nosotros; ya eres parte de la familia. ¿Tomamos un café y te presento al resto?  
(The immediate invitation to coffee and the word “familia” mirror Latin cultures that build trust through personal warmth and quick social bonding.)

4. German  
Herr Becker, herzlich willkommen bei uns! Ich stehe Ihnen jederzeit für Fragen zur Verfügung.  
(The formal “Sie” and title + surname reflect the German preference for initial distance and respect; warmth is shown through reliability, not nicknames.)

5. Arabic  
أهلاً وسهلاً بك يا أستاذة رانيا، نورتِ الفريق! إذا احتجتِ أي مساعدة، أنا بالخدمة.  
(The rhyming greeting “ahlan wa sahlan,” the honorific “ustatha,” and the light-hearted “nawwarti” blend warmth with status recognition common in Levantine workplaces.)
